# Copilot instructions for ExFace no-code platform core

## Introduction

This package is the Core of a no-code platform for business web apps called ExFace 
or Power UI (the paid version). See [general overview in the docs](../Docs/Getting_started/Introduction.md). 
The platform uses a JSON-based metamodel to describe UI, data and logic of 
apps. To run the apps, it uses PHP to generate HTML/JS front-ends and SQL or 
other data queries from that metamodel.

Each app is a separate PHP package and repo, containing its model files as JSON 
and components to extend the Core functionality written in PHP or JS. Apps, that primarily aim to extend the possibilities of the platform are called "infrastructure" apps, while those that hold models of client applications, are referred to as "payload" apps.

## Target audience

The platform is a toolbox for technically skilled business analysts to 
build custom apps for their clients. It is not a self-service tool like 
Microsoft Power Apps. Instead, it aims to provide building blocks for common 
challenges in larger business apps. It specialists will configure a metamodel, 
that puts all these blocks together into a working complex app.

### Who works works with the platform?

There are multiple groups of people, who work with the platform

- **end users** - they will use the business apps running on the platform. They 
  only see the front-end. For them it is just a web app for their every day 
  business.
- **power users** - they know how to use advanced features of the front-end 
  like advanced filters, table customization, etc. But they cannot build new 
  UIs or add new data models.
- app designers - are the business analysts, who design the apps by 
  configuring UXON models. They do not write code, but need to understand 
  data concepts. They typically can write some SQL, but do not know PHP or 
  JavaScript.
- **developers** - they write code to extend the platform, build new components. 
  They need to understand the architecture of the platform and be proficient 
  in PHP, JavaScript and other involved programming languages.

## Technical overview

The server side is built with PHP.

The UI is HTML and JS, generated by PHP facades. Using a different facade 
completely replaces the UI. Most facades are separate apps.

## Main components

The workbench works with a metamodel describing data, UI, actions, security 
- basically everything. The model is kept in a database and consist of JSON 
  objects. This particular JSON is called UXON - User Experience Object 
  Notation. See [UXON instructions](instructions/uxon.instructions.md).

### The `$workbench` class

There is a central DI container called `$workbench`, most classes receive 
through their constructor. Apart from being a regula DI container, it also 
offers explicit methods to get the most important services for simplicity:

- `getLogger()`
- `getSecurity()`
- `filesystem()`

### Metaobjects for data

All data accessible to the workbench is described in the metamodel, which is 
stored in the SQL DB of the workbench. The described data itself can be 
stored in different data sources: typically other SQL DB's, web services or 
files.

The code can access the metamodel through the PHP class 
`\exface\Core\CommonLogic\Model\MetaObject`, which is instantiated via 
factory: `MetaObjectFactory::createFromUidOrAlias()`. The metaobject is identified 
by its selector (either a UID or an alias with namespace) and provides 
methods to get information about the business object:

- `getAttributes()`
- `getRelations()`

Every object has a data source linked in the model. The object itself and 
every attribute have data addresses, that tell the workbench, where to find 
them in that data source. For SQL data the address of the object is 
typically the table name, while attribute addresses are column names or SQL 
SELECT snippets.

### DataSheets - reading and writing data

The data described by metaobjects can be read and written using DataSheets. 
Each DataSheet is basically a table - an array of rows wrapped by the class 
`\exface\Core\CommonLogic\DataSheets\DataSheet` offering methods to work 
with the data. Each row represents one instance of the object and contains its 
UID, other attributes, Excel-like calculations and even attributes of 
related objects if needed.

When a DataSheet is created, it is empty. In the code, you can tell it, what 
to read by setting filters, sorters and Aggregators or what to write by 
setting row data explicitly. The DataSheet will use QueryBuilders and 
DataConnectors defined in the metaobject to access the specific data sources,
completely hiding their complexity from the code using the sheet.

All data processed by the workbench is stored internally in DataSheets. This 
is the only way to read and write data. This ensures, that data access policies 
and business logic (e.g. object behaviors) are always applied.

### Expressions

A DataSheet is always based on one main metaobject. Every column is defined 
by an expression relative to the main object:

- an alias of an attribute of the object
- an alias of an attribute of a related object (prefixed by a relation path)
- an Excel-like formula (small php classes found in the `Formulas` folder of every app)
- a constant
- in the context of a widget an expression may also be a reference to another widget - a widget link. This is similar to referencing an excel table from another worksheet.

Expressions, that do not reference data are called "static" expressions, while 
those depending on data are referred to as "non-static" or "data-driven".

### Actions and behaviors - business logic

#### Actions

DataSheets can be passed to actions to perform business logic upon them. Each action ist a UXON prototype class, so it can be configured by the app designer.

Each action stands for a typical business task type: e.g. ReadData, CreateData, UpdateData, etc.

Actions can be triggered by users (via button) or by scheduled background processes.

#### Mappers

In addition to the inner logic of an action, `input_mapper` and `output_mappers` can be defined in the UXON to transform data before or after an action. Each mapper has a `from_object`, a `to_object` and a set of mappings in applies while transforming a from-DataSheet into a to-DataSheet. Mappings can be simple like the `DataColumnMapoing` or do complex stuff like `DataPivotMapping`, `LookupMapping` or `JoinMapping` data. There is a mapping prototype in TODO for every type of transformation.

#### Behaviors

Global business rules for a metabjects are defined in their behaviors. These are not triggered by buttons explicitly like actions, but rather react to events automatically in the background.

## Advanced features

- Filesystem abstraction
- Communication
- Mutations
- DataFlows

## Documentation

Most apps will have a `Docs` folder with markdown files. The core 
documentation has a handy table of contents in `Docs/sitemap.md`, which is a 
good entry point to look through the docs.
